summary: Justin Brennan teaches how to acquire 10-30 unit multifamily deals with minimal out-of-pocket capital ($20,000) by leveraging OPM (Other People's Money), using debt service coverage ratio (DSCR) loans at 65-70% LTV, and structuring equity raises with passive investors. He breaks down a specific $1.5M/15-unit deal model where the sponsor invests 10% equity ($50K), receives an acquisition fee ($30K), and nets only $20K out-of-pocket while controlling the asset and earning 40% of profits.
takeaways: A typical 10-30 unit apartment produces $2,500-$4,500/month free cash flow; need 3-4 deals to generate $10-20K monthly passive income
Target deal price range: $50K-$150K per unit in non-coastal markets (Midwest, Southeast, Southwest) for first deals
Commercial apartment loans are 65-70% LTV; much easier than residential because banks underwrite to building cash flow (DSCR), not personal credit
80% of deals sell during 2-3 week pre-market broker phase to investor database; only 20% reach public sites like LoopNet/CoStar—focus on direct broker relationships
Structure: Sponsor invests 10% equity, receives 40% of cash flow/profits plus acquisition fee (2-3% of purchase price), keeps 100% control
Cost segregation + bonus depreciation creates paper losses to offset W2 income; real estate professional status (700+ hours/year) allows passive loss deduction against W2 wages
Acquisition fees ($30K on $1.5M deal) + asset management fees ($1% monthly) + refinance proceeds create multiple income streams beyond monthly cash flow
Time requirement: 5-10 hours/week in beginning phase to source, analyze, and structure deals while maintaining W2 employment

skill_candidates.change: Learn DSCR loan underwriting and 65-70% LTV structuring to identify fundable deals before submission
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explains: 'The loan is easier than getting a house loan...The building is its own operating business. So, as long as that thing is producing an income substantial enough...they're going to throw the loan out the front window of the bank...because they're loaning the money to the business. You are secondary.'

skill_candidates.change: Build direct broker relationships during 2-3 week pre-market phase instead of relying on public listing sites
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin states: '80% of the time [deals] sell right there if it's a good deal...By the time it gets to Krexy, Loopnet, Zillow, CoStar...all you're looking at are leftovers...maybe 20-30% of the market' and recommends: 'direct to broker approach' by getting on brokers' email databases through property tours and relationship-building

skill_candidates.change: Commit to 5-10 hours weekly deal sourcing activity; keep daily micro-commitments (exercise, gratitude) to build self-discipline
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin advises: '5 to 10 hours a week...After your normal 9 to 5...Rather than sitting on the couch...I was on the computer looking for deals, analyzing them, writing offers' and emphasizes 'keeping the promises you make to yourself every single day...the more you don't follow those...it makes you feel pretty shitty...that's one of the hardest things we all deal with'

skill_candidates.change: Focus on $50K-$150K per unit deals in non-coastal secondary markets (Midwest, Southeast, Southwest) with mom-and-pop sellers/lenders
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin specifies: 'You've got to buy apartment deals somewhere between 50,000 per unit up to about 150,000 per unit...And you want to stay in the 10 to 30 unit apartment space for your first couple deals. Why? It's more mom and pop. You're dealing with more mom and pop lenders, more mom and pop sellers'

title: How I Bought My First Apartment Building With Just $20,000!
